claim: Sharing best practices in AI policy development through international cooperation remains a key priority for OECD members.
Inter-ministerial committees have proven effective in coordinating AI policy across different sectors.
Data protection and privacy laws in many countries have not been updated to address challenges posed by AI.
High-performance computing infrastructure is a prerequisite for training advanced AI models.
Open data platforms for AI training datasets remain underdeveloped in most low- and middle-income countries.
Secure cloud infrastructure investment is necessary to support scalable AI applications at the national level.
Integrating AI and data science into school curricula from an early age prepares future generations for a digital economy.
Only a small fraction of STEM graduates in OECD countries specialize in AI-related fields.
AI literacy programs designed to reach marginalized communities remain rare in most national strategies.
Countries should develop national digital strategies that integrate AI readiness as a core pillar.
Policies promoting competition and equitable distribution of AI gains are critical for mitigating concentration risks.
Small AI solutions offer developing countries a practical avenue to bypass traditional development barriers.
Government-funded retraining programs can help displaced workers transition to new AI-related roles.
Low-income countries face disproportionate barriers in accessing AI technologies due to compounding infrastructure deficits.
Data exchange platforms that enable secure sharing across government agencies reduce duplication and improve service delivery.
Open-source technology stacks reduce vendor lock-in and lower costs for digital public infrastructure deployment.
API-based architecture enables seamless integration between digital public services and private sector applications.
India's Aadhaar system now covers over 1.3 billion residents, demonstrating the scale achievable through digital identity infrastructure.
Dedicated digital government units staffed with skilled technology professionals improve infrastructure governance.
Digital identity systems without proper safeguards risk excluding undocumented and marginalized communities.
Multilingual interfaces in digital public services are necessary to serve linguistically diverse populations equitably.
Benchmarking digital inclusion progress against international targets helps countries identify lagging areas.
Regulatory frameworks that cap internet pricing have improved affordability in several developing countries.
Consumer protection regulations should be extended to cover digital services and AI-powered products sold online.
Approximately 400 million people worldwide still lack access to any mobile broadband network coverage.
Low-cost wireless network deployment in rural regions can dramatically expand coverage at a fraction of fiber costs.
Refugees and displaced populations are frequently excluded from national digital inclusion strategies despite acute needs.
The affordability gap is the primary reason the poorest populations cannot participate meaningfully in the digital economy.
The UNESCO Recommendation represents the first global normative instrument on the ethics of AI, adopted by 193 member states.
Liability frameworks assigning legal responsibility for harm caused by AI systems are needed in most jurisdictions.
Education systems should integrate AI ethics alongside digital literacy to prepare citizens for an AI-driven society.
Scientific research capacity in AI should be strengthened in low- and middle-income countries through dedicated programs.
AI literacy education should go beyond technical skills to include understanding of social and ethical implications.
AI technologies should promote equitable access so that low- and middle-income countries can share in their benefits.
Indigenous communities deserve special protection to ensure AI development respects their cultural values and knowledge systems.
AI systems should be designed from the outset to be accessible and usable by persons with disabilities.
No single regulatory model works in isolation; effective AI governance requires combining multiple approaches.
The maturity of a country's AI ecosystem should determine which regulatory mechanisms are most appropriate.
National AI strategies that are not periodically updated quickly become irrelevant to the evolving technology landscape.
Binding legal frameworks provide the legal certainty that AI developers need to plan and invest with confidence.
Risk-based regulatory approaches subject higher-risk AI systems to more rigorous compliance obligations.
Empowering data protection authorities to enforce AI-related regulations has been effective in the EU and Brazil.
Updating existing data protection and competition laws to cover AI-specific challenges is less resource-intensive than new legislation.
Regional data center collaborations allow smaller countries to pool resources for AI infrastructure development.
Digital literacy improvement is the most critical enabler for broader population participation in AI-driven economies.
Only 0.7 percent of new AI PhD graduates in the US and Canada choose government roles, creating a severe public-sector expertise gap.
Networks of exchange among university professors across borders can help overcome AI faculty shortages at low cost.
Less developed countries face systemic participation gaps in international AI standard-setting processes.
AI systems used in employment, education, and access to essential services are classified as high-risk under the Act.
Fines for violating AI Act prohibitions can reach up to 7 percent of a company's worldwide annual turnover.
